critique.summary: The article presents a list of 100 books identified by Le Monde as the 'Books of the Century,' featuring notable literary works from various authors. It offers free ebook downloads of these titles and includes a wide range of classic and influential literature from the 20th century.
mainPoints: Le Monde compiled a list titled '100 Books of the Century' featuring significant literary works.
The list includes internationally recognized authors such as Albert Camus, Marcel Proust, Franz Kafka, and Gabriel Garcia Márquez.
Free ebook downloads of the listed books are available through the provided collection.
The selection spans various genres and styles, reflecting diverse literary contributions of the 20th century.
critique.truthfulness: The list corresponds to a well-known cultural compilation by the French newspaper Le Monde, which published a list of the 100 best books of the 20th century based on a survey of readers and literary experts. The authors and titles listed are consistent with those widely recognized in literary circles and match the known Le Monde list. However, the article does not cite sources or provide context about the selection process, which limits its completeness. The availability of free ebooks for all these titles may be subject to copyright restrictions depending on the country, which is not addressed.
considerations: The article does not explain the criteria or methodology used by Le Monde to select these books.
It lacks information about copyright status and legal availability of free ebooks for all listed titles.
No alternative perspectives or critiques of the list are provided, which could offer a more balanced view.
The cultural and linguistic focus of the list, primarily French and Western literature, is not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Le Monde's 100 Books of the Century list, with commenters debating its French-centric bias, methodology, and the absence of certain notable authors. Some express appreciation for the list's cultural perspective, while others critique the selection and copyright issues.

comments.author: onli
comments.text:
What a strange list. Many books I'd never expect to be listed, others I'd expect to be listed are missing. So I looked up the background and indeed it's based on strange methodology, citing wikipedia: "Starting from a preliminary list of 200 titles created by bookshops and journalists, 17,000 French participants responded to the question, "Which books have stuck in your mind?" (Quels livres sont restés dans votre mémoire?"

Makes more sense like that.
comments.analysis: This comment provides context about the list's methodology, citing a large French survey, which explains the selection bias and is likely accurate.

comments.author: rixed
comments.text:
I find this other list more deserving of this title: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bokklubben_World_Library

If only because it's less french oriented, but also maybe because it starts with one of my favorite.
comments.analysis: Offers an alternative list with a broader cultural scope, highlighting the French bias of the original list, which aligns with other comments.

comments.author: Bayart
comments.text:
It's a decent list of what readers in France think of as the books to read from the 20th c., in that it holds value. Including to myself, a French citizen with odd tastes.

The general debate on what's the objective list doesn't hold weight, and I'd rather see what each corner of the world values.
comments.analysis: Provides a balanced perspective emphasizing the list's cultural specificity and subjective value, adding nuance to the discussion.

comments.author: _ache_
comments.text:
About IP. It's 70 years after the death of the author in France, so Camus (car crash in 1960) books will be PD in 2030. There is an exception for people who lost live from war (+30 years), so 2044 is the year the elevate to PD for "Le petit prince".

I don't understand that right is attached to local legislation. Like you will have access to these book before we do because of the local legislation of USA? That is a bit crazy.
comments.analysis: Accurately explains French copyright law nuances affecting public domain status, clarifying why some books are unavailable.

comments.author: throwforfeds
comments.text:
The Stranger at #1 sort of tells me everything I need to know about the list. It's a fine book, and I ended up liking it a lot more when I went back and re-read it in French many years later, but #1 of the 20th century. Yeah, not even close.

I know this is primarily a Francophone list, but not having Toni Morrison or Cormac McCarthy or so many of the great Latin American authors on it makes me wonder how much makes it into French via translation.
comments.analysis: Expresses skepticism about the ranking and highlights the absence of major non-French authors, reflecting valid concerns about the list's scope.

comments.author: gausswho
comments.text: I'm surprised to see Brave New World amongst these. The idea it presents is indeed powerful and influential, but for such a smart guy it comes across stilted and craftless. Try reading it now and it just doesn't hold up to more nuanced fiction.
comments.analysis: Offers a subjective literary critique of a specific book on the list, which is a valid personal opinion but less factual.

comments.author: Ecco
comments.text: Kinda ironic that standardebooks.org refuses non-English books but will happily promote a French ranking... I mean none of those books are actually available on standardebooks.org - at least not in their original French version.
comments.analysis: Points out an inconsistency regarding availability and promotion of non-English books, a reasonable observation.

comments.author: jackconsidine
comments.text:
> Ulysses by Joyce => 264,258 words (16 hours 1 minute) with a reading ease of 74.9 (fairly easy)

Don't want to know what difficult is
comments.analysis: Makes a lighthearted comment about book difficulty but adds little substantive analysis to the discussion.

comments.author: wackget
comments.text:
Kafka at number 3? Meh. It was incredibly dull and tiresome IMHO. I understand it's supposed to be written like a fever dream but to what end? It was simply tedious and difficult to follow.

Then again I also hated the episode of The Sopranos which has an extremely long dream sequence.
comments.analysis: Expresses a personal dislike for Kafka's work, which is subjective and does not contribute to the overall discussion about the list.

comments.author: raffael_de
comments.analysis: Concise observation about the list's French bias but lacks elaboration or supporting detail.
